@lawrenceakka I can see that it can be done, and I can understand your reluctance to add code to modules purely for debugging, however I'm still unsure if it reaches the goals I was going for. The purpose of this, was to make it easier to debug event parsing error, and this should be achieved by:

 1. Making it easy to add something to an issue that could be used to reproduce the bug exactly
 2. Making it easy for developers to replay those this and recreate the bug
 3. Making 100% sure, that what was passed into the parse function on the machine that created the error, is the same thing that is passed into the parse function of a developer machine

Your solution has a little more boiler plate, but not much, so ok for 1. If we are to not add any new code to events.py at all, then 2 cannot be achieved. The developer will have to craft the replay code himself.

The last thing is one of the more annoying and this is one of string encoding and raw vs. unicode input to logging. From a quick scan of the documentation, both for 2 and 3 it says that "msg is the format string" which I guess means that it takes python 2 str on python 2 and python 3 str on python 3. The parse function is passed bytes, and we really need to make sure those are preserved exactly. So supposed we decode and pass python 3 logging a unicode string, the it will have to be re-encoded before written to a file and with what character set, system default? This will cause problems when bug reported and developer are on different platforms. One possibly solution could be to add an extra call to debug, where the content was base64 encoded, or something like that, that is dead certain to have the same representation regardless of character encoding of the file.

This can surely be fixed by thoroughly reading the documentation and making some tests, but it was just so straight forward to simply write the bytes the parse function.

In any case, if we do decide to go purely with logging output, then I see to reason to filter in advance. The view tool can easily do that afterwards, and so could the replay code, whether it lives in events.py or in the tool.

Well, I do not want to do anything to clutter up the code. So if you still think it would be better to go with logging I'll get hacking on that.

If it's not too late, I would like to vote for the config language **not** being JSON. JSON is great  between a webserver and client-side Javascript, but its lack of comments makes it unsuitable for a config file. There are a couple "workarounds" for this, namely duplicating keys (which don't survive a deserialize/serialize operation) and naming conventions (prefixing comments with `_`, which can get out of order and detached from the statement they describe). Neither of these workarounds helps in an array.

My personal choice would be [YAML](http://yaml.org/), but even XML is a step up from JSON, strictly because of comments.

Context for those not working at Google: fontdiff is a font diffing tool that renders sample texts with HarfBuzz and FreeType into high-resolution bitmaps, using two versions of the same font. For each line of sample text, the look looks for visual differences between the renderings, and then it emits a PDF where the deltas are highlighted in color. At Google, we found this tool useful for checking the visual impact when we update a font’s source to a new version, and also when we re-build the exact same font sources with a new version of our font production pipeline. Eventually, we plan releasing the source code of this tool under the Apache 2.0 license, but the various legal reviews are still in progress so I cannot promise a specific date.
So I think this is fixed upstream, and you can build these by updating fontTools. But for the cleanest possible build environment, we should create a variable font make target. I'll work on that.

Thankfully, in this context, we aren't destroying anything.

The TMP_DIR is actually cloned from a bare git repo, it is empty except for the .git directory that was copied over.

That TMP_DIR is indeed temporary (see the mktemp a few lines up). As such, there is no history there to be lost.

The big problem here is that you can't simply do a "git checkout $GIT_SHA" as it will blow up telling you that the .git/HEAD mentions "refs/origin/master" and that doesn't exist if you didn't git push a master branch yet. So, what you end up doing, is doing a: "git checkout $(git branch -a)" followed by a "git checkout $GIT_SHA", all to end up with effectively the same thing you would have had by doing a "git reset --hard $GIT_SHA" in the first place. One way you would be in a local branch named after whatever branch they pushed checked out to the specific SHA1, the other way you end up in a disassociated branch checked out to the specific SHA1. The net effect is ambiguous after build time anyway, it's really a question of what the slug runtime sees when it tries to use that .git tree.

So, AFAIR, the only reason why we have `Chain` as a trinity-proxied service is because we cannot run `Chain.import_block()` in the same thread that runs the asyncio event loop because import_block() may block for a long time. The other approach we use to run things in separate processes (a process pool executor) doesn't work for import_block() because (IIRC) `Chain` keeps a reference to a `LevelDB` object, which cannot be pickled.

However, unlike the `*ChainDB` family, the `Chain` class is stateful, so having it as a trinity-proxied service doesn't sound like a good idea. For example, how would this work when the network-sync process is constantly updating its `Chain` instance as it imports blocks, but the IPC server's `Chain` is stuck with the state it was in when the process started. We could probably find ways to workaround that, but my gut feeling is that we need to find an alternative solution

I draw a brief diagram: the three main processes are illustrated at the top of the diagram and some *possible* sharding components/modules I suppose we need are at the bottom.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/9263930/35481312-31824da0-045c-11e8-9357-9dd022b737fd.png)

* Shard Manager
	* Upon receiving new collations, checks VMC status and applies fork choice rules.
	* Makes requests to full nodes via web3.py - could be `local-mode` or `remote-mode`
		* `local-mode`: connects directly to `Chain` of its local main chain data
		* `remote-mode`: connects to other main chain node (geth, parity, etc.)
	* Caches the lately blockhashes and `CollationAdded` logs
	* **[As a shard validator]** Upon receiving “sampled-as-collator” notification from VMC Listener, applies `CREATE_COLLATION` algorithm. 
		* Performs stateless sync via multiple interactions with DevP2P process and web3.py.
		* Collects transactions into a new collation via DevP2P.
		* Sends `add_header` tx to VMC via web3.py.
	* **[TBD]** I’m not sure about should Shard Manager be a standalone process or it could be divided into DevP2P process and EVM process. It will be clear after we figure out the interactions between DevP2P process and EVM process.
*  VMC Listener
	* Polls the VMC status and notifies Shard Manager.

`srd` is just a possible ID for Sharding Wire Protocol. (It can't be too similar to `shh`.)

Please don’t hesitate to point out any mistake.

The reason to choose pre-link is that these scripts can be expensive sometimes. What GGD is doing:

The recipes describe e.g. reference genomes, which are hosted at institutions like the NIH. Such a recipe shall allow people to easily obtain a copy of the reference genome. In order to safe space, the reference genome (which can be several GB) is not included in the package, but rather the package just delivers the code to download the genome from the NIH. This download (and eventual format conversions) happens on the fly in the pre-link script. Since it can take some time, we chose pre-link, such that different conda environments and users can benefit from the download already been done on the first installation. At least that was our idea.

Time for an experiment:

- Fresh install of Miniconda (python 3.5)
- `conda --debug install -y -f python=3.5.1=2 msvc_runtime=1.0.1=vc14_0`
- `fsutil hardlink list Miniconda3\python.exe`
- `fsutil hardlink list Miniconda3\python35.dll`

The only reason I used `-f` here is to bypass conda's auto-update, so I could installl *just* the two packages needed downgrade python. You can try it without the `-f` if you want to confirm that this is the right recipe for the job. Lots of warnings about being unable to remove the Python executables and DLLs, and empty the trash (permission denied, then not empty). And yet, _the link step succeeds_. The `fsutil` commands confirm that I am indeed hard linking to the right version of Python.

Here's the output of that conda command: https://gist.github.com/51a1bd0babb4afa25711c198dd770908

Try a different folder please, e.g. from cmd.exe run:

```
cd %UserProfile%
powershell -command "& { (New-Object Net.WebClient).DownloadFile('https://repo.continuum.io/miniconda/Miniconda3-latest-Windows-x86_64.exe', 'mc3.exe') }"
start /wait "" mc3.exe /InstallationType=JustMe /AddToPath=0 /RegisterPython=0 /NoRegistry=0 /S /D=%UserProfile%\anaconda3
%UserProfile%\anaconda3\Scripts\activate.bat
conda install -y anaconda=5.0.1 conda-build _ipyw_jlab_nb_ext_conf
```

I see it as a bug for a few reasons:

  * I found the situation puzzling. Conda instructed me to update conda with `conda update -n base conda`, but that command indicated that conda was current. Also, my initial search for conda packages (before I included the conda-forge channel) found no updates:

        (base) λ conda search "conda>=4.5"
        Loading channels: done
        # Name                  Version           Build  Channel
        conda                     4.5.0          py27_0  pkgs/main
        conda                     4.5.0          py35_0  pkgs/main
        conda                     4.5.0          py36_0  pkgs/main

  * If one doesn't intend to add the extra channel to the base environment, the warning is just noise. One could ignore the noise, but see also the next reason.

  * If the base environment's channels later acquire the newer conda version, I imagine that the warning wouldn't change to indicate that conda can be updated with the base environment's current channels.

So I 've figured out that the problem is happening if conda detects that the requested package is already present in the local package cache (~/.conda/envs/.pkgs), but not in the global cache. For some reason, under these circumstances conda tries to write to the global package cache.

**Workaround**: For me deleting ~/.conda/envs/.pkgs (or renaming it) before running `conda update` or `conda install` fixes the problem.
`post-link.sh` scripts may assume that the dependencies have been installed when they execute, that is why.

I agree that this might not be the correct place for this logic. There is already some logic around this in plan.py, but I've noticed in the past that it could use improvement, especially for the case where there are multiple package caches.

That would be a nice and clean management to pass a private index for certain pip dependencies indeed.

A workaround similar to [this](http://stackoverflow.com/a/38187246/1836416) is to pass the full package URL on the private index.

`conda package` was always labelled experimental, and never really left that stage.  conda-build is the way to *create* tarballs supported by conda.  You should be able to create a generic conda-build recipe with a [build / script](http://conda.pydata.org/docs/building/meta-yaml.html#script)  value something like

    build:
      script:  cp -R SOURCE_LOCATION $PREFIX

Once the package is built, you can invoke `conda install` with the `--use-local` flag to pull it out of the `local` conda-build channel, or use `conda install` with an absolute path to the created tarball.

As for the` --pkg-name`, `--pkg-version`, and `--pkg-build` command-line flags, I'm not sure if there's yet a way to set them dynamically with conda-build, but I know dynamic builds are on the roadmap.  If not, you might have to hard-code those in your `meta.yaml` file for now.
And also, I think the https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/6527#issuecomment-353707859 comment above is related to #6556

> we don't have filenames - we have 'dists' which are hyphen-separated string representations of the package/version/build tuples

In what way are they truly "distinct"? The reason we're moving away from using them as keys is because of the real possibility of clashing filenames: for instance, packages from two different channels with the same name and version. Passing around package-version-build triples under any circumstances seems prone to that very conflict whether they correspond to filenames or not.

Nevertheless, the code I've offered above handles both situations gracefully, if we assume that information in the package index controls when present.

@GitMensch  

There are many reasons why I would like to keep control on the compiler output. Off the top of my head I can see the following reasons:

  - I need to know the full output path even before running the compiler, for checking modification time and avoid needless compilation
  - I need to be able to specify a build directory (this is especially important for #227)
  - I also need it to implement the clean action

So I think there is no way I will drop the -o option. 

I can determine the right extension by running ``cobc --info`` and by looking at ``COB_MODULE_EXT`` and ``COB_EXEXT`` but I don't like this solution as it is not supported by old compilers and those old compiler are still lurking arround in many popular and stable GNU/Linux distributions (e.g. ubuntu still provides OpenCOBOL 1.1 from many years ago).

So the idea I have in mind at the moment is to simply add two more options to the compiler preferences:
  - executable extension: would default to ".exe" on windows and "" anywhere else
  - module extension: would default to ".dll" on windows, ".so" on GNU/Linux and ".dylib" on OSX

Thoughts?

I am not convinced this is something that should be "fixed" in VUnit. The line you list is just one example where we read or write files. I am not keen on just fixing this in one place without going all the way. There will be many places where this needs to be fixed. To handle long paths on Windows all places where we access files would have to be an absolute path with //?/ prefix. Since we also support Linux we would have to wrap all file_name operations into some function that either adds //?/ or not depends on operating system. Most Python libraries and even the Python standard libraries (https://bugs.python.org/issue18199) do not perform such work arounds on Windows. Any Windows user must live with the path limitation, it is a flaw in the operating system design. As Windows user you must thus avoid creating long paths. This is true when using any Windows software and also affects VUnit.

## configparser

- While you can hack lists onto the side with additional parsing layers after initial parsing it doesn't handle the situation we'd want (map containing a list of maps which contains lists)
- In order to add roundtripping to configparser the parser would have to be mostly rewritten
- configparser is stdlib, it would be difficult (impossible?) to convince the upstream to take such a patch.  Similar suggestions have been made for the `ast` module and were rejected.  Even if they did take the patch, we wouldn't be able to use it until python3.7 at the earliest.
- *would have to implement the parser*
- *would likely have to maintain a fork*

## commentjson
- I'd argue it has *no* implementation.  They regex out comments and have [several](https://github.com/vaidik/commentjson/issues/10), [bugs](https://github.com/vaidik/commentjson/issues/4) that indicate they desperately need a real parser.
- It doesn't look maintained or well maintained (for instance doesn't support python3)
- *would have to implement the parser*

## dumbconf
- The intent is to create a general purpose language, not a domain specific one
- For the most part, it is a strict subset of yaml, without the unnecessary complexity.  It aims to be stupid simple.
- If something like commentjson is where we want to go, dumbconf could be made _even simpler_ and not attempt to support yaml constructs.  This would mean dropping bare words, the weird indented lists, and the automatically keyed maps.  dumbconf would then be a postcard json-superset config language.  If people are ok with that, I'd be completely happy. 
- If you have issues with the spec, open issues on the project.

Thanks for also looking into this. I would like to create a library like this, like Bullet/src/BulletRoboticsSimulator. I just created a first draft to install pybullet using pip. 
(see https://pypi.python.org/pypi/pybullet/0.1.3)
Once we have the BulletRoboticsSimulator library, it will be easier/faster to use pip install pybullet, linking against those libs instead of building everything in setup.py.

Note that This 'BulletRoboticsSimulator' goes beyond just a physics engine: it loads URDF, SDF, MJCF files, can render camera images, has an OpenGL debug visualizer and client-server connection. Even without GWEN, we use OpenGL for the debug visualization and optional rendering (aside from TinyRenderer). So all together, BulletRoboticsSimulator can replace Gazebo or MuJoCo. Now, some people may want to bring their own renderer. In addition, it could be useful to have some plugin registration mechanism to inject client-code such as IMU devices or control algorithms.

1) Gwen: In the future, we could replace Gwen with another simple widgets library such as IMGUI, for the debug visualizer. We cannot just get rid of it, since some GUI is important for the built-in debug visualization.

2) We need something like glew to load OpenGL. I had to modify it, so that we can dynamically load GL1.x functions (glew doesn't do it). In addition, we can dynamically load X11 and load GLX, instead of linking against it. I want to keep this feature of dynamic loading all. So if this modified glew becomes a bother, I rather rewrite from scratch some auto-generated OpenGL bindings, since system glew doesn't solve our requirements (dynamic load X11/GLX/GL1.x).

Thanks!

Alright, this actually adds a public `forwarded` property to `BaseRequest`. It makes an effort to parse Forwarded headers as specified by the RFC:

- It adds all parameters (by, for, host, proto) in the order it finds them; starting at the topmost / first added 'Forwarded' header, at the leftmost / first-added parameter.
- It checks that the value has valid syntax in general as specified in section 4: either a 'token' or a 'quoted-string'.
- It un-escapes found escape sequences.
- It does NOT validate 'by' and 'for' contents as specified in section 6.
- It does NOT validate 'host' contents (Host ABNF).
- It does NOT validate 'proto' contents for valid URI scheme names.

            resp = await client.put_object(
                    Bucket=TEST_BUCKET,
                    Key=TEST_KEY,
                    Body=b'hello')

            print(resp)

            resp2 = await client.get_object(
                    Bucket=TEST_BUCKET,

In apt, Python 3 packages start with `python3-`, and Python 2 packages with `python-`. However, freetype and libpng are not Python packages (that's why pip won't find them). They are compiled libraries that matplotlib uses. You can find the exact names by searching on [packages.ubuntu.com](http://packages.ubuntu.com/). Note that, as pip will compile matplotlib from source, you'll need to install the `-dev` versions of packages. Alternatively, you can use apt to install matplotlib - you'll get an older version, but apt will handle the necessary dependencies.

The bit about numpy for python3 not inheriting int looks like deliberate behaviour because in python2 int is a machine integer and in python3 int is a big integer.  So that isn't a bug.

Otherwise, the isinstance(x.np.int_) problem seems similar to known issues with isinstance in autograd.  See here:

    >>> import autograd.numpy as np                                                        
    >>> type(np.int_(3))                                                                   
    <class 'autograd.numpy.numpy_wrapper.wrap_intdtype.<locals>.IntdtypeSubclass'>         
    >>> type(np.array([1])[0])                                                             
    <class 'numpy.int32'>             
    >>> type(np.array([1],dtype=np.int_)[0])
    <class 'numpy.int32'>
    >>> type(np.floor(3.14).astype(np.int_))                                               
    <class 'numpy.int32'>                                                                  

Of those 4 cases, the first is fine and the second is maybe undesirable but understandable since a type hasn't been specified.  It is unfortunate in the third and fourth cases that numpy doesn't honour the requested type.  This might be a bug, or might just be the abstraction leaking though, since numpy doesn't really hold python types internally.

```python
import autograd.numpy as np
from autograd import grad
from autograd.misc.optimizers import adam
from autograd.scipy.misc import logsumexp
import autograd.numpy.random as npr


def init_random_params(theta, rs=npr.RandomState(0)):
    scale, layer_sizes = theta
    return [
        (
            rs.randn(insize, outsize) * scale,  # weight matrix
            rs.randn(outsize) * scale)  # bias vector
        for insize, outsize in zip(layer_sizes[:-1], layer_sizes[1:])
    ]


def g_nn(inputs, params, nonlinearity=np.tanh):
    for W, b in params:
        outputs = np.dot(inputs, W) + b
        inputs = nonlinearity(outputs)
    return outputs


def kl(m1, s1, m2, s2):
    return np.log(s2 / s1) + (s1**2 + (m1 - m2)**2) / (2 * s2**2) - 0.5


def wake_sleep(inputs, inputs_f, S, theta, alpha, grad_loss=None):
    nn_params = [1e-1, [1, 10, 10, 1]]
    z = [init_random_params(nn_params) for _ in range(S)]
    init_theta = theta

    def gaussian_pdf(x, mean, sigma):
        return 1. / (
            np.sqrt(2 * np.pi) * sigma) * np.exp(-0.5 *
                                                 (x - mean)**2 / sigma**2)

    def gaussian_logpdf(x, mean, sigma):
        return -np.log(2 * np.pi) - np.log(sigma) - 0.5 * (
            x - mean)**2 / sigma**2

    def bb_alpha_energy(theta, t):
        print(theta)
        m_a, s_a, sigma = theta
        a = np.random.normal(size=(S, S)) * s_a + m_a
        m_qgp = 1. / S * np.sum([g_nn(inputs, z_i) for z_i in z], axis=0)

        mc_q = []
        for a_k in a:
            m_k = []
            for z_s, a_s in zip(z, a_k):
                m_k.append((g_nn(inputs, z_s) - m_qgp) * a_s)
            m = (1. / np.sqrt(S - 1) * np.sum(m_k, axis=0))
            mc_q.append(gaussian_logpdf(inputs_f, m, sigma)**alpha)
        mc_q = 1. / S * np.log(np.sum(np.exp(mc_q), axis=0))

        alpha_energy = 1. / alpha * np.sum(mc_q) - kl(m_a, s_a, 0., 1.)
        return alpha_energy

    if grad_loss is None:
        grad_loss = grad(bb_alpha_energy)
    opt_theta = adam(grad_loss, init_theta, step_size=0.01, num_iters=500)
    return opt_theta


if __name__ == '__main__':
    x = np.random.normal(size=(300, 1))
    f_x = np.cos(5 * x) / (np.abs(x) + 1)
    y = f_x + np.random.normal(scale=1e-1)

    m_a = np.random.normal(loc=0, scale=1e-2)
    s_a = 1e-1
    s = 1e-1
    init_theta = [m_a, s_a, s]

    S = 20
    alpha = 1.

    opt_theta = wake_sleep(x, f_x, S, init_theta, alpha)
    print(opt_theta)
```

On the other hand, the API seems to be optimized for convenient storage rather than easy/clear usage:

1. ```set_power_levels``` will trash all your previously set restrictions and permissions, so you'll almost always want to call ```get_power_levels``` first and modify its output (hence ```modify_*_power_levels```)
2. ```set_power_levels``` will control both the power level requirements for the room, as well as the power levels assigned to individual users, so it's unclear whether any given call is intended to affect the entire room or just a specific set of users in the room.

Based on the project readme, maybe the ```modify_*_power_levels``` should live in the higher level, e.g. ```room.py```?

Hi @elijahbal,

The problem is that each worker lives in a different process, so it would be hard to share a single session scoped fixture between the workers in a general manner.

For one you have the problem of serializing any object returned by a fixture so it can be sent to another process, on demand. `pickle` might seem like the obvious solution to this problem, but a lot of objects are not pickable.

Another problem, much harder IMHO, is how do you keep the object returned by the fixture synchronized among the worker processes? After all a session fixture might return a mutable object, so its state must be synchronized somehow as well.

Another problem is that one must also deal with parallelism, given that tests are now running in parallel in separate processes. The resources probably will need to be synchronized using a lock.

Those are the reasons from the top of my head why it would be hard to implement the proper scope rules in a way that session scoped fixtures are only instantiated once per-session, as opposed to once per-worker like it is today.

Hi Chris,
The short answer is an ATECC508A device used for the unit tests can not be used with the node-auth-basic example and vice-versa. You'll need to use a new ATECC508A device for each example.

The unit tests, node-auth-basic, and other example projects often require different configurations for the ATECC508A device. However, once configured, it can not be reset and reconfigured, due to security concerns.

This limitation does make it challenging to experiment with. We often recommend one of the AT88CK101 socket boards (https://www.microchipdirect.com/product/search/all/ck101) and a handful of blank parts when going through the initial experimentation and development phases.

Regards,
-Ben

I'm guessing Gentoo support for the Apache plugin is going to be a pain in the #*&$*#...

It seems Gentoo is a little bit strange in terms of its [configuration](https://wiki.gentoo.org/wiki/Apache#Configuration)..

httpd.conf is filled with constructions like the following:

> <IfDefine SSL>
> LoadModule ssl_module modules/mod_ssl.so
> </IfDefine>

And in the `conf.d` file for the init.d script, all defines are specified like `-D SSL` for the command line.

So, even if you change `apache2ctl` to `apache2`, one runs into the problem of missing all those extra `-D` switches when [configurator.py wants to do its configtest](https://github.com/letsencrypt/letsencrypt/blob/apache-portability/letsencrypt-apache/letsencrypt_apache/configurator.py#L1316):

Well, in that specific case, `configtest` is non-existant in `apache2`, but when we change that to `-t`, the Gentoo equivalent, it gives the following error:

> ERROR:letsencrypt.le_util:Error while running apache2 -t.
> 
> AH00526: Syntax error on line 15 of /etc/apache2/vhosts.d/vhosts/example.com.conf:
> Invalid command 'SSLEngine', perhaps misspelled or defined by a module not included in the server configuration

This because `-D SSL` is missing from the command. A solution would be to extract the variable `APACHE2_OPTS` out of `/etc/conf.d/apache2` and append it to every `apache2` command.

@pde @bmw I'd love to hear your opinions on this IPv6 issue. I'm a bit on the edge where we should error out from validating IPv6 only vhost. This is mostly about the code health in the long run. If we do the checks in client, we have multiple options where to error out:

1) Do not list the vhost as available at all - needs a lot of checking in code all around, which can be cumbersome to remove when we have IPv6 support
2) Error out before challenge - this will interrupt the flow, and is harmful in a case where user wants to validate multiple domains, one of which being IPv6 only
3) Let the server fail the challenge - same downsides as above, and additionally generates unnecessary load on the servers.

Another option would be to ignore the IPv6 definitions altogether, which would then result into broken SSL vhosts generated by apache plugin.

4) If `-h` or `--help` is provided on the command line and `letsencrypt` is not installed (e.g. there isn't a venv), continue execution but print a message saying that help for the `letsencrypt` program cannot be provided until it is installed. So the consumer starts up, gets your assignment and then asks the broker for committed offsets for  topic qa_gcdb_shipment_queue partition 2 in group maxwellConsumer, but the broker responds with offset -1 which means there are no committed offsets for that combination.

```
%7|1515001264.714|OFFSETFETCH|rdkafka#consumer-1| [thrd:main]: 172.31.230.155:9092/1: OffsetFetchResponse: qa_gcdb_shipment_queue [2] offset -1
```

There are generally two reasons for this:
 * there were no prior commits for the given topic,partition,group.id
 * there were prior commits but they happened longer ago than the offset topic's retention time, thus they have been removed.

Either way the client needs to know what to do when there are no committed offsets to use, so it looks at `auto.offset.reset` and looks up that offset. In your case that is the end of the partition and thus no messages are seen until new messages arrive.

Do note that this consumer will only commit actually messages it has seen, in this case it has not seen any actual message though, only the end of the partition, so nothing is committed.
As soon as a new message arrives it will commit the offset for that message though.

@ProgVal What you were doing before was definitely a hack. Consider 2.4.2's behavior for other similar strings:

```python
>>> from dateutil import parser
>>> parser.parse('now', fuzzy=True)
datetime.datetime(2016, 3, 8, 0, 0)
>>> parser.parse('tomorrow', fuzzy=True)
datetime.datetime(2016, 3, 8, 0, 0)
```

I suspect that you may have been fooled because the default behavior if a string contains a date used to be to return the "default" date, which defaults to, "today" (notice how `'now'` didn't even return the current time?).

If you want to keep the old behavior, just wrap it in a try-catch block:
```python
from dateutil import parser
import datetime

try:
    dt = parser.parse('now', fuzzy=True)
except ValueError as e:
    dt = datetime.datetime.now()
```

In the 2.6.0 release, I'm planning on switching the errors over from `ValueError` to custom error classes so they can be more easily differentiated in error flow, but for now you can parse the message if you need to distinguish between causes of the errors.

We've seen enough time-desynched servers ruining everything (not in this project yet, but still).

I suggest that we request a new, simple, unauthenticated, remote resource, `/getCurrentTime`, which would return time accurate to one second. Then we will use that time to put metadata in the params dict which will say what the _server time_ was when the file was synched/uploaded. Then a `sync` with `--keepDays` would again use the _server time_ to compute which files should be deleted and which should not be. The unit tests will be run with a time set to 1970-01-01 (using [freezegun](https://github.com/spulec/freezegun) or alike) to make sure we don't rely on a (possibly very bad) time on the server.

On another note, if `sync` with `--keepDays` is started with a time difference between the system clock and the Backblaze clock which exceeds one day, then we will fail immediately unless a special flag is provided. This is to make sure that we don't delete too much data due to a failing system clock on an existing system which has its clock set to a long time ago.

My experience with DFU is that I update 12 devices in batch.  I pre-load 12 USB device on a hub and attach the hub.  Every factory on the planet needs a DFU tool that supports more than one DFU device at a time.  Only an engineer or a user can get away with 1 at a time limit. So, I wrote a Python based DFU tool that didn't explode when more than one device was in DFU mode.

Other use cases:
- Multiple of the same USB to serial devices that do not have a unique serial number. (example below)
  - I think that windows device driver locking can force a constant comport assignment.
  - Mac You're out of luck!
  - Linux, BSD, or other posix???
- Matching embedded FTDI chips behind a hub buried inside of equipment.
  - Higher bits on location ID are the same as you progress down the USB bus.
  - USB 3.0 port_numbers is a tuple that extends similarly.
- Checking if upstream hubs are MTT to prevent communication bottlenecks with many USB ports.
  - Someone has taken my nice 12 port industrial hub out of a setup and swapped in a 4 port that didn't have MTT nor sufficient power, but the station only needed 4 ports... :boom: 
- Erring out if a user changes physical USB ports. (alternative methods exist, but not as simple)

USB 3.0 specifies a ports parameter that has the same effect.  location ID happens to map exactly to USB 3.0 port_numbers.  My example if location ID is not the only important fact.  USB 2.0 drivers don't have that value.

No we did not. This is not exactly an asyncio issue. It works fine. The situation might improve slightly with a faster loop and certainly works on fast hardware and an idle loop. However, in practice, an asyncio process with "only" 10% CPU utilization fails to write more then a few kBaud of serial data because of small buffers. Instead the StreamWriter will buffer all the data and the process will run out of memory after some minutes if you are not careful.

In synchronous writes the user space send buffer is used and the kernel buffer never underruns. Even with a 90% busy asyncio loop. I guess this is a more general non-blocking I/O issue (at least on linux).

@alex-r-xiao 
Let me give you an example: the following is one possible set of **margin scores** produced by a 2-iteration ensemble. We assume `num_class=3`. (The numbers are all made up, for the sake of illustration.)
```
Tree 0 produces  +0.5
Tree 1 produces  +1.5
Tree 2 produces  -2.3
Tree 3 produces  -1.5
Tree 4 produces  +0.1
Tree 5 produces  +0.7
```
Even though we trained for 2 iterations, we have a total of 6 decision trees. This is because, for **multi-class classification**, XGBoost will produce `[number of iterations] * [number of classes]` trees. Let's walk through to see how these margin scores get transformed into the final prediction.

1. Group the trees into **output groups**.
There should be as many output groups as there are label classes. For this example, there are 3 label classes, so there are 3 output groups. The member trees are grouped as follows:
```
Output group 0:  Tree 0, Tree 3
Output group 1:  Tree 1, Tree 4
Output group 2:  Tree 2, Tree 5
```

2. Compute the sum of margin scores for each output group.
```
Output group 0:  (+0.5) + (-1.5) = -1.0
Output group 1:  (+1.5) + (+0.1) = +1.6
Output group 2:  (-2.3) + (+0.7) = -1.6
```
The vector [-1.0, +1.6, -1.6] is not a proper probability distribution, as the numbers don't add up to 1. However, even this is quite useful: the sum for output group 1 is the largest, so the second class is the most probable class.

3. Transform the sums using **the softmax function**.
To apply what is known as [softmax](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Softmax_function), first apply the exponential function (`exp`) to every element in the vector.
So we start with
```
[-1.0, +1.6, -1.6]
```
and compute
```python
np.exp([-1.0, +1.6, -1.6])
```
which gives us
```python
[ 0.36787944,  4.95303242,  0.20189652]
```
Then normalize the vector by dividing it by its element sum:
```python
np.exp([-1.0, +1.6, -1.6]) / np.sum(np.exp([-1.0, +1.6, -1.6]))
```
giving a proper probability distribution:
```python
[ 0.06661094,  0.89683221,  0.03655686]
```
We interpret this vector as showing 6.7% probability for the first class, 89.7% for the second class, and 3.7% for the third class.

Efficiently counting models for propositional formulas is not quite 
trivial. An incremental improvement over your algorithm is to
run dual propagation: take each equality from the model, 
assrt (=> tracking-predicate-i (= var-i value-of-var-i-in-model)),
and assert the negation of the formula you are checking.
In this case you end up with an unsatisfiable formula 
modulo the tracking-predicates. An (minimal) unsatisfiable core is a
reduced set of equalities that are required to evaluate the formula to true.
variables that don't participate in the core are don't cares and you can
multiply the counter by 2^DomainSize for these variables.

Unfortunately, this alone will do little for you. 
If this was a SAT solver, it would be able to leverage
equivalence finding procedures inside of the solver to factor out
equivalent literals. For bit-vectors and other domains, you 
do have the option to convert this to a pure propositional formula
and then count models for this. 

An ad hoc approach, but not so easy, is to leverate a feature
in Z3 that allows to learn implied equalities. It is not mainstream
so currently only exposed over the C API.


    Z3_lbool Z3_API Z3_get_implied_equalities(Z3_context c,
                                              Z3_solver  s,
                                              unsigned num_terms,
                                              Z3_ast const terms[],
                                              unsigned class_ids[]);

You can use this to find equal variables, and therefore only count
models for one variable in each class.

I am going to close this as an "issue". It isn't a bug and the ways to address it are open ended.

The benchmark uses the theory of arrays, and ends up instantiating arrays using auxiliary functions that are defined in the model. These auxiliary functions are not defined with the instantiation, so the solver may be unable to use them in finding unsat.

For example, a model may define the function k!79, which is used in an (_ as-array k!79) expression.

(define (k!79 x!0)
  (if (= x!0 5) (mk___rep (mk___split_fields natural!val!2 natural!val!0 natural!val!1))
  (if (= x!0 (- 8851)) (mk___rep (mk___split_fields natural!val!2 natural!val!2 natural!val!7))
    (mk___rep (mk___split_fields natural!val!2 natural!val!0 natural!val!1)))))

By the time these instantiations are used, there is no information what k!80 and k!79 are.

In the end, the theory of arrays decides that it cannot for sure say whether the formula is sat because there is no complete support for as-array.

I don’t see a simplistic fix here as Z3 does not have lambdas at this point. Lambdas could be very nice for several reasons, but also a somewhat substantial change. In lieu of lambdas we could expose what these functions k!80 etc. are when the instantiations are used. It amounts to asserting the macro axiom for these functions when the instantiations are invoked. This could introduce regressions. 
While MBQI wasn't designed with the theory of arrays in mind, it rather assumes that arrays are encoded directly using quantified formulas, we would of course like to get the integration of the various features to play along.

> I'm not sure about the note on not using it for training; the current form would at least be differentiable.

As far as I know, it is not used for training, while differentiable. See [this](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1702.08699.pdf) which introduces a loss function for training which is near `jaccard_index` but not exactly the same. 

> The docstring does not make clear if the objective also applies to multi-class problems.

Done!

> It would be nice to include references to a paper or two that use it.

Jaccard index is the same as Intersection-over-Union (IoU) metric which is being used in semantic segmentation/Object Detection tasks. PASCAL VOC uses this metric for evaluation.
See [this](http://www.bmva.org/bmvc/2013/Papers/paper0032/paper0032.pdf) for reference.

After looking some more, apparently installing requirements directly from git in `setup.py` has been deprecated. If installing/updating Lasagne won't directly alter Theano, I think pinning to a specific commit is not so important, and maybe it's better just to tell people to use Theano master (and depend on our tests to quickly reveal any incompatibilities).

My new suggestion is to remove the Theano requirement from `setup.py`, and add a check on import that Theano is installed and `theano.__version__ >= KNOWN_GOOD_VERSION`, raising a warning with instructions to install from github if not.

I ran across this same type of event, and added in a clean call to the `save` override I made on many models in my apps.

```
def save(self, **otherstuff):
    self.full_clean()
    super().save(**otherstuff)
```
(replacing "otherstuff" with the actual args involved in this).
ref:  https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.11/ref/models/instances/#saving-objects


The full_clean method does all the model field definition's checks, as the db itself does nothing at all besides table relationships.
ref: https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.11/ref/models/instances/#django.db.models.Model.full_clean

Hi @gregpinero,

I usually create a custom format and override create_dataset for that use case. But I abuse it saving FK values no matter if dry_run is set or not. Anyway it may be of use to you:

```
class CustomFormat(XLS):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.headers = ['define headers here']

    def create_dataset(self, in_stream):
        xls_book = xlrd.open_workbook(file_contents=in_stream)
        dataset = tablib.Dataset()
        sheet = xls_book.sheets()[0]

        dataset.headers = self.headers
        for i in moves.range(1, sheet.nrows):
            values = sheet.row_values(i)
            ... recover data, process values, 
            dataset.append(values)
        return dataset

```

You are right. I just want to mention that in `CFGAccurate`, at every conditional branch point, all branches will be taken in an _non-derministic_ order, regardless of the satisfiability of each branch. Therefore, regardless of whether `r11` equals to `0x11` or `0x12` in the initial state, you will see one of the following possibility: a) `nxt2` branching to itself, or b) `nxt2` branching to `_start`.

`CFGAccurate` will definitely use values in the initial state to resolve indirect jumps. For example, if you have `jmp r11` somewhere, you will see a branch from that instruction to a new block whose address is the value of `r11` in your initial state.

Within my own projects I've been using django-apptemplates: https://pypi.python.org/pypi/django-apptemplates/

This adds a template loader that namespaces 'extends' to a specific app. So I can do:

    {% extends "admin:admin/base_site.html" %}

in my template and override only the blocks I need without getting a circular 'extends' loop.

This allows me to reduce the copy+paste from Django's base.html and means I'm much less likely to cause breakage when Django changes it's own html.

django-apptemplates is very small and simple so if another dependency is an issue we could include it in it's entirety.

@myz8110 原因是这样的，`logger` API并没有直接在 `from rqalpha.api import *` 中暴露。

是通过如下方式注入进去的:

```python
def create_base_scope():
    import copy

    from . import user_module
    scope = copy.copy(user_module.__dict__)
    scope.update({
        "logger": user_log,
        "print": user_print,
    })

    return scope
```

之所以这么做的原因是不想覆盖用户 `print` 函数，我们重写了print函数，但仅限于用户的"策略代码" 中。 而如果直接通过API 暴露的话，会引起Python 2兼容性，以及一些由于上下文而产生的问题。 

也就是说，您额外写的文件通过 `from rqalpha.api import *` 的方式是无法直接使用logger的。

在下一个版本中，我们将 logger 通过API 暴露出去了，下一个小版本更新后您就可以使用这种方式来输出日志信息了。

如果您现在就想使用的话，也可以通过如下方式引入 `logger`

```python
from rqalpha.utils.logger import user_log as logger
```

@will-cern So I think there are two possible ways of doing this:

1. Make a new splitter that does exactly what you want and creates the subjobs of a single master job based the names/folders of the given local input. This will do what you want but be a bit inflexible as regards specifying input data, merging output, etc.

2. Submit a Task that creates a master job per input name/folder. This will mean more than one job but they would collected under a single Task which is (almost) as good and Ganga would handle everything about this as well - you'd just ask if a specific Task is complete rather than a specific Job. The benefit of this is that there's less dev work do to and the your have a lot more options with regards the output (as I say, merging, collating, etc.).

My thoughts are:

* I'm not super concerned with the current 27.5MB download size, *but*
* I do think it would be nice to have a better framework for collecting standard data sets, including larger data sets

Some of the other issues on matminer for example pertain to the Jarvis data set which would be nice to have as a standard data set, without increasing the download size of matminer. Even the elastic tensor data set in matminer should have an updated version available since the number of points in MP is now >6000 rather than the ~1000 in the example.

So having a framework for getting additional data sets without having it bloat matminer itself would be very useful. Some comments:

(i) Whatever the framework is, it should be easy to use and seamless. Ideally without a lot of installation quirks and headaches. Scientists are typically bad at installing things and have little patience when things break.
(ii) Probably I'd want to leave the current data sets in there rather than migrate them for the time being, at least until it's really proven that people can use an alternate data set downloader without issues. So the framework would be more for additional data sets.
(iii) Maintaining the framework for hosting data should not be complicated or break-able

Incidentally, we previously played with git-lfs for hosting data and examples. It broke rules 1 and 3 though.

> Ad: Helper classes: I didn't write them with intention to stay there for long time/forever, but only as simple temporary solution which can be easily extended or replaced later when we will have more use cases for them. I think that for now they could help us to write tests, ie. create and delete repository by on simple method call.

Aye, I understand the intent behind writing them. But in my opinion, they don't quite qualify as "simple". To use or understand code that makes use of `Repository`, a user must answer questions like:

* When a `Repository` object is instantiated, what happens to the arguments passed in? Are they saved as instance attributes? (No. They're saved in the `data_keys` attribute.)
* Do all arguments correspond to values accepted by the server, or are some keywords reserved by the object itself? (All args are passed to the server.)
* Do the methods like `create_repo` and `delete_repo` mutate the object they're called on, or do they not? (They do mutate the object.)
* Do methods like `create_repo` and `delete_repo` return anything? (No.)
* Why is it that `__init__` and `create` accepts an arbitrary set of kwargs, but `update_repo` explicitly constrains what values can be passed in via its signature? (I don't know.)
* The methods include "repo" in their name. Does this mean that I have to learn a new set of method names for every single object? (Yes.) Does this mean each object is unique in other ways? (Yes.)

So please, do not add helper objects right now. A simple set of constants (and *maybe* functions) is enough.

Mind you, I'm expecting unit tests for any globally accessible code. Code has a nasty habit of sticking around longer than expected.

@Oxyrus that's correct. 

In this case, you can implement your own Middleware (http://docs.graphene-python.org/en/latest/execution/middleware/) and have different rules for different fields in your schema.

Other option would be to have two endpoints. One for non-logged users served by GraphQLView with query containing fields that should be exposed to everyone. And another endpoint served by TokenAuthGraphQLView with queries and mutations available to logged in users. 
I like it as it makes it easier to distinguish between public and private APIs, but GraphQL specification recommends having only one endpoint, so it might not follow the specification fully.

The third option would be to authenticate non-logged users as guests and provide access to certain fields based on your authorization rules and framework, but that's basically the same as having authentication checks in all fields.

        h_embeddings = embed(h_inputs)

        h_embeddings = tf.expand_dims(h_embeddings,axis=1)
        h_embeddings = tf.tile(h_embeddings,(1,max_length,1))

        outputs = tf.concat([h_embeddings,b_embeddings],axis=1)

        for layer in range(self.mlp_layers):

                outputs = tf.layers.dropout(outputs,rate=self.dropout_rate,training=self._training)
            outputs = tf.layers.dense(outputs,self.num_neurons[layer],activation=self.activation,kernel_initializer=self.initializer,
                                         name = "hidden{}".format(layer+1))

            outputs = self.activation(outputs,name= "hidden{}_out".format(layer+1))

        return outputs

    def _loss(self,y_,predicts,lengths,max_length):

        predicts = tf.reshape(predicts,shape=(-1,max_length,2))
        masks = tf.sequence_mask(lengths,maxlen=max_length)
        masks = tf.cast(masks,dtype=tf.float32)
        masks = tf.expand_dims(masks,axis=2)
        masks = tf.tile(masks,multiples=[1,1,2])
        predicts = tf.multiply(predicts,masks)
        predicts = tf.reshape(predicts,shape=(-1,2))
        xentropy = tf.nn.softmax_cross_entropy_with_logits_v2(labels=y_,logits=predicts)
        loss = tf.reduce_mean(xentropy,name="loss")

        return loss

        self._accuracy = accuracy

Thanks for raising this issue and agree that it needs to be fixed!  Would you be willing to submit a PR to address this?

We may want an API in the future that is more page-based. For that to work, we would have some platform-defined constants like `OT_PLAT_FLASH_ERASE_PAGE_SIZE` and `OT_PLAT_FLASH_WRITE_PAGE_SIZE`, and change the erase function to be something like `otPlatFlashErasePageAtAddress(uint32_t addr)`, which would be understood to erase a single page at the given address.

Also, page erases tend to take a significant amount of time. I would make the page erase operation non-blocking and add an additional function called something like `otPlatFlashStatus()` which returns if the flash erase operation is finished or not. Writes (which should not trigger erases) and reads are usually pretty quick.

As I mentioned in a code comment, it isn't clear what the behavior of the write operation is intended to be. I'd recommend it be defined to match what the actual hardware does: only clear bits, never set them. This requires that the caller manually erase pages before writing them if they want to set bits. This is a good idea because you can use this behavior of flash devices to your advantage for implementing atomic writes.

We should also clearly define that this API should never allow firmware to be overwritten, and that the effective address space starts at address zero.

I'm trying to keep the `PROP_PHY_*` properties as general purpose as possible. As such I tend to prefer the general widespread convention rather than force the limited 802.15.4 definitions on everything.

Alternatively, I'd be happy to define a `PHY_MODE_802_15_4_PROMISCUOUS` that is defined to be the same numerical value as `PHY_MODE_MONITOR`.

Of course, if there is consensus among the maintainers to follow the 802.15.4 convention, I'll do that. 

On an unrelated note, I'm considering moving this property from the `PHY` section to the `MAC` section.

TxTotal = TxBeacon+TxBeaconRequest+TxData+TxDataPoll+TxErrCca+TxRetry

TxBeacon+TxBeaconRequest+TxData+TxDataPoll=TxAckRequested+TxNoAckRequested

TxAckRequested - TxAcked = number of packets that have not been acked

RxTotal=RxData+RxDataPoll+RxBeacon+RxBeaconRequest+RxOther+RxWhitelistFiltered+RxDestAddrFiltered+RxErrNoFrame+RxErrUnknownNeighbor+RxErrInvalidSrcAddr+RxErrSec+RxErrFcs+RxErrOther.

Another way could be to modify the generic type caster and have another ``else`` branch that tries this conversion (perhaps with an extra `` if (std::is_pod<Type>) { }`` to avoid generating useless code for non-POD classes).

The tricky part would be to know when that conversion is applicable. Here is one thought about that: NumPy supports attaching metadata to a type.
```
>>> np.dtype('<i8', metadata={'hello': 'world'}).metadata
mappingproxy({'hello': 'world'})
```
This could be used to cache the ``class_<>`` type object of the underlying POD type. Specifically, ``dtype::of<>`` could call ``detail::get_type_info(const std::type_info &)`` to get the ``class_`` object and stick it into the metadata when creating a new dtype. 

Then in the ``generic_type_caster`` in ``cast.h``, there could be quick to see if the object is a NumPy array. If so, the next test would verify that the dtype metadata matches the caster's type. The last step would run the operation you mention. This scheme obviously requires that types are bound before corresponding dtypes are generated.

However, there is one major catch: What I *really* want to avoid is pulling ``numpy.h`` into the main set of header files. NumPy support is a very useful feature, but it's not needed by many (most?) applications.

So we'd need to find a way to do all of these steps without relying on ``numpy.h``, and without executing any code that would import ``numpy`` on the Python side.

> I think I prefer the approach from stackoverflow which defines a custom version of `is_copy_constructible` (perhaps in the `pybind11::detail` namespace?). This could then be partially specialized in `stl.h`. What do you think?

That's basically the approach here, except the specialization is right along with the general case, using one specialization that should work for any stl container types.

Putting the override in `stl.h` won't work (`stl_bind.h` doesn't depend on it), and putting it in `stl_bind.h` won't work, either, because we don't actually know what the container type is: it's just a template argument given to the `bind_vector` or `bind_map` function.

We also can't just list it for all stl types, because there are stl types like `std::deque` which are perfectly usable with `bind_vector`, but are never `#include`d anywhere in pybind11.

We are aware of manual plugin, it works pretty well in one pass scenario. We also implemented something like this. But regarding usage with Ansible we got this issue ticket:
https://github.com/EnigmaBridge/certbot-external-auth/issues/3

It basically says Ansible already has a lot of plugins for DNS auth so the idea was to use this Ansible capability to perform DNS validation. As I understand it, it cannot be done in one pass as certbot cannot simply invoke Ansible DNS plugin. Thats the reason we would need to serialise challenges and finish the validation later. The issue author suggested something like this:

```
- shell: certbot -d example.com <more args>
  register: result
  set_fact:
      certbot_out_json: "{{ result.stdout | from_json }}"

- your_cloudprovider:
      dns:
          domain: _certbot-challenge.example.com
          type: txt
          value: "{{ certbot_out_json.token }}"

- shell: dig -t txt _certbot-challenge.example.com
  retry: 5
  delay: 60

- shell: certbot --resume --session-id {{ certbot_out_json.session_id }} <more args>
```

To be clear, the problem you experienced here is due to the downgrade in the version of `certbot`/`letsencrypt` you were using. This will be a common problem, however, as people upgrade from Ubuntu 14.04 to 16.04. There is no certbot/letsencrypt package on Ubuntu 14.04 so people use `{certbot,letsencrypt}-auto`. When they upgrade to Ubuntu 16.04 and try to use the OS package, the process fails due the error you linked above. The Certbot team including our Debian package maintainer have [asked Ubuntu](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/python-letsencrypt/+bug/1610030) to consider upgrading to a newer version, however, they have been unresponsive.

In general, I feel that OS packages (including the out of date `letsencrypt` package in Ubuntu) provide a better experience for the user over the `-auto` scripts. If you're installing certbot/letsencrypt on your server for the first time, I think we should recommend Ubuntu's `letsencrypt` package over `certbot-auto`. However, since more and more `certbot-auto` users on 14.04 will be updating from to 16.04 this is perhaps something we should consider.

Other possibilities include:
* Explaining this complexity to Ubuntu 16.04 users on https://certbot.eff.org
* Resolving #1706 
* Work (harder) with Ubuntu to upgrade the package

I agree this a rough spot in using Certbot right now and we should do something to smooth it out.

It's the first time I write in the forum, sorry if make any mistake, or this is not the appropriate place.

I am working on image segmentation, and I found this thread; If i'm right, the recommended approach atm is the one proposed by @alykhantejani [here](https://github.com/pytorch/vision/releases/tag/v0.2.0). I took the idea, and re-implemented `torchvision.transforms` in such a way that its default behavior is the same as usual, but if you pass a tuple `(image, target)` you get back `(transform(image), transform(target))`, and both are transformed in exactly the same way. For instance, in the `Resize` class, I do the following:
```
if target is not None:
    return F.resize(img, self.size, self.interpolation), \ 
              F.resize(target, self.size, self.interpolation_tg)
return F.resize(img, self.size, self.interpolation)
```
taking care that the interpolation in the `target` argument defaults to `NEAREST`, which is what one often wants in these situations.

I have shared this re-implementation, which I call ''paired transforms'' in [this repo](https://github.com/agaldran/torchvision_paired_transforms), and documented it with a couple of jupyter notebooks. I have few experience in software development, the code may not be bug-free, or it could be quite sub-optimal, but I thought I could share it in case it is useful for someone trying to apply this kind of transforms while minimally modifying their code.

Cheers,

Adrian

I'm a little skeptical of having a set of keys that belong to a driver. For example, if this is done as part of the driver table, then this would double the number of Redis commands that we have to do, right (every time you add a key, you also have to update the set)?

Alternatively, if each Redis shard keeps its own set for each driver and we rely on custom Redis modules, then it will be much harder to replace Redis with other stores in the future (if we want to).

As for driver heartbeats, there are a lot of ways this could go wrong. E.g., if the user's application code does something for 5 seconds without releasing the GIL then the driver could be accidentally marked dead. But primarily, we already rely on node manager to detect driver deaths when they disconnect and to clean up that way, and I'd prefer to have a single mechanism to do this instead of two.

Maybe the right way to do actor handle ID generation (for pickled handles) is the following. Suppose we have

```python
actor_handle = Actor.remote()

# Defining this remote function, which closes over actor_handle, will
# cause actor_handle to be pickled. The remote function will be broadcast
# to all the workers and unpickled there.
@ray.remote
def f():
    actor_handle.method.remote()

f.remote()
```

Instead of generating a random actor handle ID on each worker when the remote function is broadcast, maybe we should instead leave the actor handle in an "uninitialized" state, and then whenever a `f.remote()` task executes, we generate a new actor handle ID deterministically from the actor ID and the current task ID. In other words, we want it to be roughly equivalent to calling `f.remote(actor_handle)`. Doing this will cause a proliferation of actor handles, so we'll need to make sure that is handled efficiently.

The documentation you linked offers an _example_ of configuring the use of hugepages system-wide. I.e., other applications, not just Ray, may have access to this pool of hugepages configured. Thus, `--object-store-memory` behaves in the same way it does without hugepages, namely, the total object store memory allocated shall not exceed the limit provided via `--object-store-memory`. The only undesirable interaction I can think of is the case when system-wide hugepages pool size is smaller than the object store memory limit specified with `--object-store-memory`.

Good question re: page size. Note that the object store will work with any mount point provided. Thus, it may work with 2MB as well as 1GB sized pages, as long as they were mounted using hugetlbfs, and the mountpoint -- provided to the plasma store on startup. This provides the flexibility for the plasma store to work with whatever existing configuration may already be in place for hugepages on the target platform.

The problem seems to be that the primary Redis shard is overwhelmed (it's CPU utilization seems to grow linearly with the number of workers, reaching 100% utilization with around 15000 workers) even when no tasks are running and the cluster isn't doing anything. The CPU utilization is a combination of two things.

1. Each worker has an `import_thread`, which calls

    ```
    worker.import_pubsub_client.psubscribe("__keyspace@0__:Exports")
    ```

2. Local schedulers and plasma managers send frequent heartbeats to the primary redis shard.

I think the issue is that `psubscribe` is causing all of the local scheduler heartbeats to be more expensive than they should be (presumably the channel name is checked against all of the patterns). However, we aren't actually using patterns in these psubscribes, so the fix should be easy.

https://streamlink.github.io/cli.html#cmdoption-player-continuous-http
The documentation itself is fine and the parameter you're talking about is a multi-character parameter, which requires a double hyphen if we want to keep all parameters in [Unix style](https://serverfault.com/questions/387935/whats-the-difference-betwen-the-single-dash-and-double-dash-flags-on-shell-comm).

It is just the anchor that doesn't include the whole parameter name.
The anchor format that's being used here is
`"cmdoption" + "-" + parameterWithoutAnyLeadingDashes`
and not
`"cmdoption" + parameter`.

Interesting approach! I was imagining something using a wake-up fd, but I think you're right and `threading.Event` could work too, and more simply.

This version does have a problem though: if the main loop goes to sleep for 5 seconds because there's nothing to do, then that's fine... but the watchdog still fires. We only want the watchdog to fire if we spend a long time in the task-running part of the loop; it should ignore the IO-handling part.

Of course, doing that between two threads without race conditions is not trivial... Here's my idea. Keep two counters: one that we increment whenever we start running user tasks, and one that we increment whenever we stop. So when we're in the task running part of the code, the counter values are unequal, and when we're in the io-handling part they're equal. Only the main thread mutates these counters; the watchdog thread observes them. The watchdog should fire if it ever makes two observations that are at least X seconds apart, where the values haven't changed, and are unequal to each other. That combination means that we're stuck in a user task.

Since python's memory model is pretty strongly sequential and consistent, and assignment is atomic, I think we can skip any locks on these counters, which is nice.

With this setup, a correct-but-slightly-inefficient implementation would be:

```python
while True:
    orig_starts = self._starts
    orig_stops = self._stops
    time.sleep(X)
    if orig_starts = self._starts and orig_stops = self._stops and orig_starts != orig_stops:
        # watchdog fires
```

The downside to this implementation is that it wakes up every X seconds even if the main thread is sleeping, which isn't a huge flaw but it does waste some power and besides, it's just not elegant. Can we do better?

```python
while True:
    self._event.clear()
    orig_starts = self._starts
    orig_stops = self._stops
    if orig_starts == orig_stops:
        # main thread asleep; nothing to do until it wakes up
        self._event.wait()
    else:
        self._event.wait(timeout=5)
        if orig_starts = self._starts and orig_stops = self._stops:
            # watchdog fires
```

And I think whenever we start running tasks we do `self._started += 1; self._event.set()` (in that order), and when we stop running tasks we can skip the event and just do `self._stopped += 1`.

Maybe this would be simpler if we used a `Condition` directly? `Event` is a thin shim around it, and not adding much value here...

As mentioned in the [javadoc for invoke](https://github.com/ninia/jep/blob/v3.7.0/src/jep/Jep.java#L515), the name passed to invoke must be the name of a global variable. In this case "add" is the name of a global variable and "cal" is the name of an attribute on that object. "add.cal" is not the name of a global variable so it does not work, this is the expected behavior. The alternatives you suggested are acceptable, if you prefer invoke then you would need to use an eval to get the function into a global variable like this:

```
jep.eval("tmp = add.cal")
jep.invoke("tmp", x, y)
jep.eval("tmp = None")
```

`Issue` objects retrieved with `gl.issues.list()` don't have a `notes` manager. This reflects the GitLab API.

You need to create a `ProjectIssue` object to access its `notes` manager:

```
for issue in issues:
    project_issue = gl.projects.get(issue.project_id, lazy=True).issues.get(issue.iid, lazy=True)
    project_issue.notes  # now this works
```
The `lazy` avoid making extra calls to the API.

The problem is when overloads have different arguments or returns.  

```
void func1( int i )
void func1( int i, String s)
int    func1( String s)
```

would be close to impossible to specify.  Thus it is best if we only add this for non-overloaded in the first cut.

I do agree that if a method has only one overload that it would be fairly trivial to generate a signature.  I will just need to see how the specification is applied to the actual python function.  

In jpype version 0.7, the methods both bound and unbound are stored directly in the class structure.  We would need to add a signature hook which would call a piece of python code to create the signature meta data and cache it.
